title: The Second Sunday of Advent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: December 5, 2021

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Second Sunday of Advent falls on December 5, 2021, in the Christian calendar's season of preparation for Christmas. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would likely include Isaiah 40:1-11 (comfort my people), Psalm 85:1-2, 8-13, 2 Peter 3:8-15a, and Mark 1:1-8, focusing on John the Baptist's role as forerunner and themes of preparation and repentance. The liturgical color for Advent is purple, and the second candle of the Advent wreath (representing faith or preparation) would be lit during this service.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the post-General Convention period following its triennial General Convention held in July 2021 in Baltimore, which had addressed numerous resolutions on social justice, climate change, and church governance. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Bonnie Perry (elected in 2015), was navigating the ongoing pandemic's effect on congregational attendance and participation. Saint John's Cathedral itself, as the seat of the Diocese of Colorado, was a prominent institution in the Mountain West region of the Episcopal Church.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical early December weather patterns with temperatures in the 40s-50s Fahrenheit and occasional snow in the foothills. The Denver metropolitan area was focused on holiday preparations and the upcoming end-of-year holiday season. Colorado's COVID-19 case numbers were rising in early December 2021, coinciding with the Omicron variant's emergence and spread across the United States, affecting holiday gathering plans statewide.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The primary national story was the rapid emergence and spread of the Omicron COVID-19 variant, first reported in South Africa in late November and now being detected across the U.S., causing new uncertainty about holiday gatherings and winter infection rates. President Joe Biden was navigating ongoing inflation concerns and infrastructure bill implementation while preparing for the 2022 midterm elections. The Kyle Rittenhouse verdict (acquittal on most charges related to the Kenosha shooting) had been reached on November 19, 2021, and remained a major divisive national topic in late November and early December.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Omicron variant's rapid global spread was the dominant international story, with countries worldwide implementing travel restrictions and health measures in response to the new COVID-19 variant. Afghanistan remained in humanitarian crisis following the Taliban's August 2021 takeover and the chaotic U.S. withdrawal, with winter conditions threatening vulnerable populations. China-Taiwan tensions were escalating, with increased Chinese military activity around Taiwan in late 2021 raising geopolitical concerns.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in early December 2021 was marked by cautious holiday optimism mixed with pandemic anxiety, particularly as the Omicron variant threatened the first "somewhat normal" Christmas since 2019. The nation was experiencing significant cultural polarization over COVID-19 policies, vaccine mandates, and holiday gathering guidance. Entertainment and sports continued normally (the NFL season was in full swing, holiday movies and programming were launching), but the underlying mood was one of uncertainty—Americans wanted to celebrate but remained wary of new restrictions or case surges.
